old_claim.note: Fiber Optics provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Fiber became an important transmission medium, but it was not required by the heterogeneous operational Internet of January 1983.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction (p. 1) describes packet radio and satellite networks; Appendix D (p. 16) lists operational gateways joining ARPANET, SATNET, PRNET, UCL Net, RSRE Net, RCC Net, and Comsat DCN Net.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 801 documents the ARPA Internet interconnecting packet-radio, satellite, ARPANET, and other networks through gateways.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: An internetwork operating across radio, satellite, and legacy packet-network links demonstrates medium independence; fiber optics can scale later links without defining the 1983 milestone.

ontology_before: The Internet node treated one later high-capacity physical medium as a direct prerequisite for a heterogeneous internetwork.
ontology_after: The Internet node is medium-independent at its direct boundary and depends instead on packet switching plus the TCP/IP protocol environment.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-internet-operational-boundary: internet no longer directly depends on fiber_optics.
would_reject_if: Primary records establish that every network participating in Full Internet Service in January 1983 required fiber-optic transmission.
short_reason: RFC 801 documents the operational internetwork across satellite, packet-radio, and other non-fiber links, making fiber optional rather than prerequisite.
